title: Shout for Joy
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Shout for Joy - Pastor Tito Lyro - Psalm 33.txt

outline.point: I. Praising God Leads the Believer to Hope (vv. 1-3)
subpoints: A. Praise arises from the righteous — it is beautiful and fitting
B. Song invites corporate participation and expression of the full range of affections
C. The cyclical pattern: praise leads to hope, which leads to further praise

outline.point: II. We Praise God Because of Who He Is (vv. 4-5)
subpoints: A. His word is right — morally complete, not merely accurate
B. All his works are done in faithfulness to himself and his covenant promises
C. The earth is full of his hesed — his steadfast, covenantal love

outline.point: III. We Praise God Because He Is Creator and Ruler (vv. 6-9)
subpoints: A. God spoke and all things came into existence effortlessly
B. He commands and creation stands fast — nothing is accidental
C. Humanity instinctively stands in awe of creation; how much more of the Creator

outline.point: IV. We Praise God Because He Rules Humanity (vv. 10-17)
subpoints: A. No human plan or military power can thwart the counsel of the Lord
B. Blessed is the nation whose God is the Lord — the church is that people
C. The Lord sees all and fashions hearts individually; all are accountable to him

outline.point: V. We Praise God Because He Loves and Delivers His People (vv. 18-22)
subpoints: A. The eye of the Lord rests in loving care upon those who fear him
B. Complete trust and surrender to the Lord — our soul waits for him
C. The character of God — especially his sovereign control — leads to praise, which moves us to hope

summary:
Preaching from Psalm 33, Pastor Lyro traces the psalm's structure and its central insight: that praising God moves the believer toward hope, not the other way around. The psalm is titleless, composed for corporate worship, and its 22 verses unfold a cyclical pattern in which each generation is encouraged to praise the Lord for past and present evidences of his love, and to expect that he will give further reason for praise. The sermon walks through five grounds for praise: the nature of God himself (vv. 4-5), his effortless creation (vv. 6-9), his sovereign rule over nations (vv. 10-17), his loving care for his people (vv. 18-22), and the resulting posture of waiting trust expressed in the psalm's final verses.

Pastor Lyro stresses that the Christian is constitutionally a singing person — not because circumstances are always favorable, but because singing is an act of faith that shapes the heart. He draws on Psalm 40's picture of conversion — God lifting a person from the miry pit, placing them on a rock, and giving them a new song — as the archetypal image of Christian experience. He also presses back against the common objection regarding evil in the world, asking instead what accounts for all the goodness, beauty, and self-sacrifice that exists: the answer is the Creator God of the Bible. The sermon closes with a lengthy quotation from Spurgeon's Morning and Evening devotional, emphasizing that the presence of Jesus is sufficient comfort even in the deepest distress.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Psalm 33

If you're able, you may remain standing. This morning we're going to look at Psalm 33. We're going to return to our series on 1 John, Lord willing, on the 20th of February. This morning we're going to consider Psalm 33, and then on the 13th of February we're going to consider Psalm 12, and then we'll jump back into 1 John.

The title for the sermon this morning is "Shout for Joy." Psalm 33. This is the word of our Lord.

Rejoice in the Lord, O you righteous! For praise from the upright is beautiful. Praise the Lord with the harp; make melody to Him with an instrument of ten strings. Sing to Him a new song; play skillfully with a shout of joy. For the word of the Lord is right, and all His work is done in truth. He loves righteousness and justice; the earth is full of the goodness of the Lord. By the word of the Lord the heavens were made, and all the host of them by the breath of His mouth. He gathers the waters of the sea together as a heap; He lays up the deep in storehouses. Let all the earth fear the Lord; let all the inhabitants of the world stand in awe of Him. For He spoke, and it was done; He commanded, and it stood fast. The Lord brings the counsel of the nations to nothing; He makes the plans of the peoples of no effect. The counsel of the Lord stands forever, the plans of His heart to all generations. Blessed is the nation whose God is the Lord, the people He has chosen as His own inheritance. The Lord looks from heaven; He sees all the sons of men. From the place of His dwelling He looks on all the inhabitants of the earth; He fashions their hearts individually; He considers all their works. No king is saved by the multitude of an army; a mighty man is not delivered by great strength. A horse is a vain hope for safety; neither shall it deliver any by its great strength. Behold, the eye of the Lord is on those who fear Him, on those who hope in His mercy, to deliver their soul from death, and to keep them alive in famine. Our soul waits for the Lord; He is our help and our shield. For our heart shall rejoice in Him, because we have trusted in His holy name. Let Your mercy, O Lord, be upon us, just as we hope in You.

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Precious God, we pray that as we consider this passage, it would speak to us — that you would consider the foolishness of the message proclaimed by a defiled speaker, and would come and meet your people's hearts by the power of your Spirit. We pray that you would point us to Christ even as we look at Psalm 33. In Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: Why Psalm 33?

I wonder why this psalm? Why Psalm 33? The only answer I have is that it's the next psalm. I try to work my way eventually through the whole book of Psalms, and that's just the next psalm. I hadn't spoken on this psalm before.

This is one of those psalms that we really can't figure out when and why it was written. There's no title on it — no inspired title attached to this particular psalm. We can't really figure out when and where it was written, as opposed to, say, Psalm 34. If you look at the following psalm, it has a very detailed title: "A Psalm of David, when he pretended madness before Abimelech, who drove him away, and he departed." Psalm 33 has no such title. In the New King James, the heading "The Sovereignty of the Lord in Creation, History, and Redemption" is not part of the psalm — that's something the editors put there.

This psalm is titleless. It is obviously designed for corporate worship. There's a lot of plural here — a lot of "we" and "us" — designed to be used in the service of the church as we are doing this morning. It is one of only four psalms in Book One that lacks a title.

William VanGemeren, an Old Testament scholar who focuses on the Psalms, said this concerning Psalm 33: "The structure reveals a cyclical pattern by which each generation is encouraged to praise the Lord for past and present evidences of His love." Consequently, each generation is to expect the Lord to give further reason for praise as the eyes of faith are fixed on Him and on a renewal of His love.

## I. Praising God Leads the Believer to Hope (vv. 1-3)

The first thing this psalm teaches us is that praising God moves the believer to hope — which is a little counterintuitive. We usually think that praise arises from a heart that's hoping and rejoicing in God. But the Bible also teaches that praising God leads us to hope and to joy. Which means there is no time in our lives when we're not ready to praise God.

Look at verses 1 through 3: "Rejoice in the Lord, O you righteous!" The ESV translates the first line as "Shout for joy in the Lord, all you righteous" — that word "rejoice" could be translated as "give a ringing cry to the Lord." Just be loud for the Lord in praising Him.

And notice that these praises are in the form of song in verses 2 and 3: "Praise the Lord with the harp; make melody to Him with an instrument of ten strings. Sing to Him a new song; play skillfully with a shout of joy." These verses speak of instruments and singing.

The Christian cannot not sing. The Christian cannot worship God without singing. Singing is part of who you are as a Christian. I've had people tell me, "I don't sing. I just listen to people singing in the worship service." That's not what Christians do. Christians sing. Doesn't matter how bad your voice is. The old King James translation of Psalm 100, "Make a joyful noise to the Lord," is a comfort to a lot of us.

A Christian sings because he's happy. A Christian sings because she's sad. A Christian sings because she wants to grow in grace. A Christian sings because he is lacking hope in the Lord. A Christian sings because that's what Christians do.

Keith and Kristyn Getty, in their book *Sing! How Worship Transforms Your Life*, write: "Our churches are called to be strong in the Lord and in His mighty power. We are not people cowering in a corner, nursing wounds of defeat. We are a city on a hill, shining in a dark world, people of victory, joy-filled, with the powerful presence of the Holy Spirit, serving a Savior who shall reign forever. When we sing, it is a battle cry of hope for the wounded, the weary, the lost. Sing of Jesus. Sing of your Lord and Savior and greatest friend. Sing yourself strong. Sing the church strong. Sing up and sing out."

## II. We Praise God Because of Who He Is (vv. 4-5)

The major reason the Psalmist gives us for praising God — the major reason the Holy Spirit gives us — is the nature of God himself. Who He is. That's what you see in verses 4 and 5: "For the word of the Lord is right, and all His work is done in truth. He loves righteousness and justice; the earth is full of the goodness of the Lord."

Why should we praise the Lord? Not because of circumstances, but because of who God is.

His word is right — and here it doesn't necessarily mean correct or accurate, though the word of the Lord is that. It means morally upright, complete. We don't have to doubt it. It is exactly what it says. And all his works are done in truth, meaning done in faithfulness. In all that God does, God is faithful to himself and to the promises he has made to his people in the covenant of grace.

Because of his faithfulness to himself, he is faithful to us — because he promised us to never leave us nor forsake us. Psalm 27, according to verse 12, tells us that even when the most sacred human relationship fails, when father or mother forsake us, the Lord is always going to be with us.

Believing this is true has tremendous practical everyday implications. If we are praising the Lord for who he is, then we will see that the good things and the bad things that happen in our lives only happen because a sovereign God who is faithful to those who trust in him appointed them to happen. It doesn't remove the pain from hard things. It doesn't completely explain why suffering comes into our lives. But it does give meaning to suffering, because we know that the God who promises to work all things for the ultimate good of shaping us to be like Jesus Christ caused that thing to happen. That's the beauty of Romans 8:28, read with verse 29 attached: God is working all things for the good of those who love him and are called according to his purpose — so that they may be conformed to the image of the Lord Jesus Christ.

And the psalm describes God as one who loves righteousness and justice, because they are part of his character. "The earth is full of the goodness of the Lord." The word translated "goodness" here is not the typical word for goodness in the Old Testament. It's actually a covenantal word that expresses God's heart toward those with whom he has covenanted — the same word used in verse 22, translated "mercy," and often in the ESV translated "steadfast love." That's God's attitude toward his people.

So the earth is full of evidences of God's steadfast love for his people. This matches Psalm 19: "The heavens declare the glory of God, and the firmament shows His handiwork."

## III. We Praise God Because He Is Creator and Ruler (vv. 6-9)

The psalm also teaches us that we praise God because the Lord is the Creator and Ruler. Look at verses 6 through 9. The Bible goes out of its way to show how effortlessly God created all that we know. The stars, the planets just popped into existence at his command.

"By the word of the Lord the heavens were made, and all the host of them by the breath of His mouth." He spoke and everything came into existence. "He gathers the waters of the sea together as a heap; He lays up the deep in storehouses" — just by the sound of his voice, as in verse 7. Nothing is accidental in creation. "He commanded, and it stood fast" (verse 9). Everything was created exactly as God intended.

Unlike the theology of the ancient nations, where different gods ruled over different parts of nature, the God of the Bible rules over all things because he created all things. Remember the ten plagues — God was demonstrating to Egypt: I am the God of all things. I'm sovereign over these man-made deities.

Humanity is instinctively in awe of creation. As VanGemeren says: "Rainstorms, hurricanes, flash floods, subterranean waters, and the mighty oceans inspire awe because of their power. How much greater should we stand in awe of the Lord who created all those things!"

People get caught up with the problem of evil — how can a good God allow evil in this world? But we can get so caught up with that that we forget all the good and beauty that exists in this world. What accounts for altruism? What accounts for selflessness? What accounts for beauty? Evolution doesn't, because evolution says the key for existence is self-preservation — that does not account for beauty, does not account for goodness. The only thing that accounts for it is what the heavens declare: God, the God of the Bible, who created all things.

## IV. We Praise God Because He Rules Humanity (vv. 10-17)

Here we have a transition from creation in general to concentrate on humanity specifically. The Psalmist teaches us that regardless of who the superpowers are, regardless of the military arsenal, no one can deter the Lord from executing his plans.

"The Lord brings the counsel of the nations to nothing; He makes the plans of the peoples of no effect. The counsel of the Lord stands forever, the plans of His heart to all generations" (vv. 10-11).

During the French Revolution, the new republic tried to eliminate the Sabbath by making a ten-day week. The main reason was to stop people from worshiping God. They knew it was intrinsic to God's plan of salvation that his people would gather every seventh day to worship him. Guess what happened? How many days do we have in a week? Seven. Because no power can undo what God has decided to do.

Proverbs 19:21: "There are many plans in a man's heart, nevertheless the Lord's counsel — that will stand." Proverbs 21:30: "There is no wisdom, no understanding, no counsel against the Lord." And Isaiah 46:9-10: "I am God, and there is no other... My counsel shall stand, and I will do all my pleasure."

Is that worth praising him for? Surely shout for joy! Our God is gathering a people that cannot be numbered from every tribe, every tongue, every ethnic group, to praise him for all eternity. We shout for joy because of that.

The Psalmist tells us that those who submit to God are blessed: "Blessed is the nation whose God is the Lord, the people He has chosen as His own inheritance" (v. 12). The word "nation" here is broader than what we think — it refers to a people's group. Ultimately, I think this "nation" and "people" is the Church of the Lord Jesus Christ. Acts 19:6, 1 Peter 2:9-10 makes that clear: "But you are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, His own special people, that you may proclaim the praises of Him who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light."

## V. We Praise God Because He Loves and Delivers His People (vv. 18-22)

The psalm narrows even further — from all mankind to those who specifically fear the Lord. "Behold, the eye of the Lord is on those who fear Him, on those who hope in His mercy, to deliver their soul from death and to keep them alive in famine" (vv. 18-19).

Notice the total surrender to the Lord on the part of those who fear him. The eye of the Lord being upon someone means that his loving care is upon that person. And as a result of all of this, we hope in the Lord who loves us: "Our soul waits for the Lord; He is our help and our shield. For our heart shall rejoice in Him, because we have trusted in His holy name. Let Your mercy, O Lord, be upon us, just as we hope in You" (vv. 20-22).

Praising God because of his sovereign character will lead us to hope. Listen to what Spurgeon says about these last verses in his devotional *Morning and Evening*, from the morning of July 2nd:

"Blessed is the fact that Christians can rejoice even in the deepest distress. Although trouble may surround them, they still sing; and, like many birds, they sing best in their cages. The waves may roll over them, but their souls soon rise to the surface and see the light of God's countenance. They have a buoyancy about them which keeps their head always above the water and helps them to sing amid the tempest... God is with me still. To whom shall the glory be given? Oh, to Jesus! It is all by Jesus. Trouble does not necessarily bring consolation with it to the believer; but the presence of the Son of God in the furnace fills his heart with joy. He is sick and suffering, but Jesus visits him and makes his bed for him. He is dying, and the cold, chilly waters of Jordan are gathering about him, but Jesus puts his arms around him and cries, 'Fear not, beloved.' As the departing saint wades through the stream... the same voice sounds in his ears: 'Fear not! I am with thee; be not dismayed, for I am thy God.' And as he nears the borders of the infinite unknown... Jesus says, 'Fear not! It is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom.' Thus strengthened and consoled, the believer is not afraid to die... Truly, the presence of Jesus is all the heaven we desire."

And that's why we hope in God. The character of God — especially his sovereign control of all things — leads us to praise him. And our praise moves us to hope. We hope in God because he has demonstrated that he is utterly faithful to us in the life, death, and resurrection of our Lord Jesus Christ. Because of that, we shout for joy.

Let's pray together. Father in heaven, we thank you for this beautiful psalm. We thank you for preserving for us in an inerrant way this word from a brother centuries ago. We pray that your Spirit would have his way with your people through your word. We pray that you convict us in our hearts and lead us to praise that will lead us to hope. In Jesus' name, amen.
